sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Chest infection is a general term used to describe an infection that affects the lower parts of the respiratory system, specifically the lungs and airways (bronchi). Many different bacteria, fungi, and viruses can cause infection, but the two main types of chest infections are bronchitis (typically viral) and pneumonia (typically bacterial).

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Fever, Rigors
- a persistent cough, can be productive with yellow/green phlegm or coughing up blood
- Wheezing, breathlessness and/or rapid and shallow breathing
- Chest pain or tightness when taking a breath
- Feeling confused and disorientated

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider off site transfer (home) if suitable
- Tachycardia (≥100 bpm), Tachypnoea (≥22 rpm), reduced on air saturation (≤ 94%), hypotension (≤ 90mmHg), acute onset confusion should raise concern for sepsis
- Severe illness signs: Dyspnoea, chest pain, altered mental status, hypoxia, signs of sepsis or organ dysfunction and need urgent review and management off site
- Patient history of Asthma (or other COPD) and/or smoking is important for management, i.e., antibiotic choice +/- steroids +/- Ventolin
- It is perfectly reasonable to advise patients to purchase their own medications (paracetamol, ibuprofen, cold and flu, nasal sprays, antihistamines, etc.) from the village store where appropriate

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital Signs
- Auscultation
- Consider antipyretic medications only if temperature causing discomfort
- Consider “over the counter” analgesia
- Secondary Survey if required COMMONPRESENNCE

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider blood draw for i-STAT test in severe cases (Blood Urea Nitrogen, BUN and lactate where applicable)
- Consider IV fluids where required
- Consider oral antibiotics (Amoxicillin, Augmentin Duo Forte) only in consultation with on- call doctor
- Consider IM/IV antibiotics in severe cases (Ceftriaxone) only in consultation with on-call doctor

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Cough suppressants SHOULD NOT be supplied/administered if production is evident
- i-STAT Chem8+ BUN reference range 3-140 mg/dL (normal range 7-20mg/dL)
- i-STAT Lactate of > 2 mmol/L should prompt review
- The CRB-65 and CURB-65 scores are both used to assess the severity of community- acquired pneumonia (CAP). 1 point for each:
  - Confusion (new disorientation in person, place, or time, etc.)
  - BUN testing (where available)
  - Raised respiratory rate (30 breaths per minute or more)
  - Low blood pressure (systolic less than 90 mmHg or diastolic 60 mmHg or less)
  - Age 65 years or more COMMONPRESENNCE
